steps.dialogue: You've already practiced strategies for comparing fractions. [event:698637683be8ab15]When denominators [event:698637809899c461]match, you count the pieces.  [event:6986386614be717b]When [event:6986387d9b663861]numerators match, you compare piece sizes. Those approaches have worked with every problem we've explored.
But here's a new challenge. What happens when the [event:69863bce5afeb430]numerators don't match, and [event:69863c79179ee5eb]denominators don't match? Let's see.

Here are two fractions, [event:698e1f1f93869551][fraction numerator=3 denominator=4]three fourths[/fraction] and [event:698e2007f22e9854][fraction numerator=2 denominator=6]two sixths[/fraction]. Before we compare them, let's look at the middle point of both bars. [event:698fc5ea815f0b14]Notice this line showing where halfway is on each bar. Is [fraction numerator=3 denominator=4]three fourths[/fraction] more or less than [fraction nummerator=1 denominator=2]one half[/fraction]?

prompt.text: Is [fraction numerator=3 denominator=4]three fourths[/fraction] more or less than [fraction nummerator=1 denominator=2]one half[/fraction]?

step.dialogue: Look once more at where the shading stops. Does it reach past the middle line?

metadata.description: Highlight the halfway point on the 3/4 bar, show that 3 parts extends past it

step.dialogue: Let's think about it. Half of 4 parts would be 2 parts. You have [event:698662ff143d6d86]3 parts shaded. Is 3 more than 2?

metadata.description: Animate pointing to the halfway mark, then to the shading showing it extends past

step.dialogue: Let's walk through this together. This bar [event:69866491bf3b3d45]has 4 equal parts. Halfway would be [event:698664bf7088f23e]2 parts. But [event:6986660ece9e910d]3 parts are shaded, that's [event:698665ebc5365a89]more than halfway. See how it goes past the middle line?

on_correct.dialogue: Right. [fraction numerator=3 denominator=4]Three fourths[/fraction] goes [event:69865cc95f3d1f7a]way past the halfway point. It's almost the whole bar.

steps.dialogue: Please check where [fraction numerator=2 denominator=6]two sixths[/fraction] sits next. Is [fraction numerator=2 denominator=6]two sixths[/fraction] more than or less than [fraction nummerator=1 denominator=2]one half[/fraction]?

prompt.text: Is [fraction numerator=2 denominator=6]two sixths[/fraction] more or less than [fraction nummerator=1 denominator=2]one half[/fraction]?

step.dialogue: Not quite. Look again at where the shading stops compared to the middle line.

metadata.description: Highlight the halfway point on the 2/6 bar, show that 2 parts doesn't reach it

step.dialogue: Half of 6 parts would be 3 parts. You only have [event:69867482aca8f705]2 parts shaded. Does that reach halfway?

metadata.description: Animate pointing to the halfway mark, then to the shading showing it doesn't reach it

step.dialogue: Follow along with me this time. This bar has [event:6986756544f31b03]6 parts. Halfway would be 3 parts. But only [event:698675f08fbdb1c2]2 parts are shaded, that doesn't reach the halfway point. [event:6986764f2bee848f]So [fraction numerator=2 denominator=6]two sixths[/fraction] is less than halfway.

on_correct.dialogue: Right. [fraction numerator=2 denominator=6]Two sixths[/fraction] [event:698672f59272ce3e]doesn't even reach the halfway point.

steps.dialogue: So [fraction numerator=3 denominator=4]three fourths[/fraction] is past halfway, and [fraction numerator=2 denominator=6]two sixths[/fraction] doesn't reach it. What does that tell you? Which fraction is greater?

prompt.text: Compare [fraction numerator=3 denominator=4]three fourths[/fraction] and [fraction numerator=2 denominator=6]two sixths[/fraction].

step.dialogue: Take another look and think about what we just figured out. Which fraction went past the halfway line?

metadata.description: Highlight both bars with the benchmark line, emphasize 3/4 is above and 2/6 is below

step.dialogue: [event:698f8870175d0345][fraction numerator=3 denominator=4]Three fourths[/fraction] is way above halfway. [fraction numerator=2 denominator=6]Two sixths[/fraction] is below halfway. Which one is greater?

metadata.description: Animate selecting 3/4, highlight the comparison with arrows showing positions relative to benchmark

step.dialogue: This time, let me show you. If [event:698f88c76d684b2f][fraction numerator=3 denominator=4]three fourths[/fraction] is above the halfway line and [event:698f88d59d481c64][fraction numerator=2 denominator=6]two sixths[/fraction] is below it, then [fraction numerator=3 denominator=4]three fourths[/fraction] must be [event:698f8935e081c7f6]greater. When one fraction is [event:698f89f5b2f83508]past halfway and the [event:698f8b2c6dced981]other isn't, the [event:698f8b819083ba48]one that's past halfway is greater than the other.

on_correct.dialogue: Yes! By comparing [event:698f7f4a91fce0a0]both fractions to the halfway point, we can tell that [fraction numerator=3 denominator=4]three fourths[/fraction] [event:69962048fdcc6abf] is greater. You used the halfway point as a reference to compare these fractions.

steps.dialogue: Your usual strategies wouldn't have worked here. We needed to take a different approach. Today, we'll explore the new strategy we just introduced.
